subsystem: QEMU audio backend — the OSS (Open Sound System) playback path. oss_write() is the HWVoiceOut->write callback that drains PCM audio the guest has queued and either copies it into an mmap'd DMA emulation buffer (hw->buf_emul) or write(2)s it to the OSS device file descriptor. It is the leaf that moves guest-produced audio samples out to the host sound device.
provenance: Identified from the QEMU audio subsystem naming: the HWVoiceOut / OSSVoiceOut hardware-voice types, the OSSVoiceOut downcast idiom, the hw->buf_emul / hw->pos_emul / hw->size_emul mmap-emulation fields (defined in audio/audio_int.h), and the oss_logerr / oss_get_available_bytes / advance helpers all belong to QEMU's audio/ossaudio.c. The banner comment /* sample: primevul_c_train_2847_fixed */ marks this as the post-fix revision of a PrimeVul C sample; the file tag names CWE-369 (divide-by-zero).
trust_boundary: The guest VM drives the emulated sound card. The guest chooses the audio format/geometry (which sizes the emulation buffer) and produces the PCM stream and the number of bytes to play, so the amount `len` and the buffer-geometry state (hw->size_emul, hw->pos_emul) are ultimately guest-influenced host state. The mmap branch trusts that hw->size_emul is a non-zero power/size reflecting a real allocation of hw->buf_emul; if setup left size_emul == 0 (mmap disabled/failed, or a zero-sized geometry the guest negotiated) the modulo on line 16 divides by zero. The source pointer `buf` and length come from the audio core; the destination geometry is the security-relevant contract.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The HWVoiceOut->write backend entry point. It receives the generic hardware-voice handle `hw`, a pointer `buf` to the PCM bytes to emit, and the byte count `len`. Line 4 downcasts the base HWVoiceOut to the OSS-specific OSSVoiceOut, which is legal because QEMU embeds HWVoiceOut as the first member of OSSVoiceOut so the pointers alias. `pos` (declared here, used later) tracks how many bytes were actually written in the non-mmap path. The function returns the number of bytes consumed, which the audio core uses to advance its ring buffer.

Selects the memory-mapped DMA path when the OSS device was opened with mmap support. Line 9 clamps the requested `len` down to the number of bytes currently free in the device's mmap region as reported by oss_get_available_bytes(oss) — this MIN() is the fix that prevents writing more than the device can currently accept. Line 11 snapshots the clamped amount into total_len so the function can report the full (pre-drain) count back to the caller even after the loop decrements `len` to zero.

The ring-copy loop into the emulation buffer. Each iteration computes to_copy as the smaller of the remaining `len` and the distance from the current write cursor pos_emul to the end of the buffer size_emul (line 13) — this is the classic 'don't copy past the physical end, wrap instead' pattern. Line 14 memcpy's to_copy bytes from `buf` into hw->buf_emul at offset pos_emul. Line 16 advances the write cursor modulo size_emul so it wraps back to 0 at the end of the ring. Lines 17-18 advance the source pointer and decrement the remaining count. The loop repeats until all clamped bytes have been placed, wrapping at most once because to_copy is capped at the tail distance.

After the ring copy completes, the mmap path returns total_len — the full number of bytes accepted into the emulation buffer (the pre-loop snapshot), telling the audio core how much of its queue was consumed. Control never falls through to the write(2) path when mmapped is set.

The non-mmap fallback: write the PCM straight to the OSS device fd. pos accumulates bytes successfully written. Each iteration computes pcm = advance(buf, pos) — a pointer into buf offset by the bytes already emitted — and calls write(oss->fd, pcm, len) with the remaining count. bytes_written is a signed ssize_t so it can carry the -1 error return. On error (line 29) it logs via oss_logerr unless errno is EAGAIN (a transient 'device busy' that is intentionally silent) and returns pos, the count actually drained so far.

On a successful write, pos advances by bytes_written (line 37). If the kernel accepted fewer than the requested `len` bytes (a short write, line 38) the loop breaks — the device is momentarily full and the caller will retry later. Otherwise line 41 subtracts the fully-written amount and loops to push the remainder. The function finally returns pos, the total bytes handed to the OS. Note the comparison on line 38 mixes signed bytes_written with unsigned `len`, though at that point bytes_written is guaranteed >= 0 by the earlier check.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This is the central hazard and the reason the sample is tagged CWE-369. The modulo divides by hw->size_emul. If the emulation buffer was never successfully sized — size_emul == 0 (mmap allocation failed, the buffer was torn down, or the guest negotiated a zero/degenerate geometry) — the `% 0` on a size_t triggers integer division-by-zero, which raises SIGFPE and crashes the QEMU process: a guest-triggerable host denial of service. The clamp on line 9 does not save this path: if oss_get_available_bytes returns non-zero while size_emul is 0, `len` stays non-zero, the loop body runs, and line 16 faults on the very first iteration. The implementation MUST guarantee that the mmap branch is only reachable when buf_emul is a live allocation with size_emul > 0 (i.e. mmapped implies a validated, non-zero-sized region), or must guard `if (!hw->size_emul) return 0;` before entering the loop.

Unsigned pointer-arithmetic hazard. size_emul and pos_emul are size_t; the subtraction hw->size_emul - hw->pos_emul underflows to a near-SIZE_MAX value if the invariant pos_emul <= size_emul is ever violated (e.g. a stale pos_emul left over from a larger prior geometry, or size_emul == 0 with pos_emul == 0 giving 0). The MIN(len, ...) normally caps to_copy at the remaining `len`, so a huge underflowed operand is masked — but that safety depends entirely on `len` itself being bounded to the true free space in buf_emul by line 9. If size_emul == pos_emul == 0 the subtraction is 0, to_copy becomes 0, and with len still non-zero the loop would spin without progress — except line 16 divides by zero first and crashes. The implementation must maintain 0 <= pos_emul < size_emul at all times so this distance is a valid, in-range tail length.

The destination write. Its safety rests on two contracts the header/setup must uphold: (1) hw->buf_emul actually points at an allocation of exactly hw->size_emul bytes, and (2) pos_emul + to_copy <= size_emul so the copy stays inside that allocation. to_copy is bounded by the tail distance on line 13, so given a correct, consistent (buf_emul, size_emul, pos_emul) triple this cannot overflow the destination. The residual risk is a mismatch between size_emul and the real allocation length of buf_emul (out-of-bounds write, CWE-787) or buf_emul == NULL (NULL-pointer memcpy, CWE-476, though to_copy would be 0 in the degenerate size_emul==0 case). This is why size_emul is the crucial invariant: it governs both the divide on line 16 and the bound here.

This clamp is the fix that bounds how much is copied into the mmap region, so its correctness is load-bearing. If oss_get_available_bytes over-reports (returns more than the free space in buf_emul, e.g. because it derives its answer from the OSS device's play_ptr/GETOSPACE rather than from buf_emul's own geometry), then `len` can exceed the room between pos_emul and size_emul across wraps, and the loop could write more total bytes than the ring holds before pos_emul is re-synced — an out-of-bounds write. The implementation must ensure the available-bytes accounting is consistent with buf_emul/size_emul, not just with the kernel's DMA pointer.

Signed/unsigned comparison hygiene. bytes_written is ssize_t and `len` is size_t; C promotes bytes_written to unsigned for the comparison. Here it is benign because the earlier `if (bytes_written < 0)` guard guarantees bytes_written >= 0 at this point, so the unsigned reinterpretation preserves its value. It is worth confirming, however, that no future edit removes that guard — without it a negative bytes_written would compare as a huge unsigned value and mis-drive the short-write logic (CWE-195/CWE-697).

summary: A guest-facing QEMU OSS audio write path whose one sharp, latent defect is a division-by-zero: line 16 computes pos_emul modulo hw->size_emul with no guarantee that size_emul is non-zero, so any state where the mmap emulation buffer is unsized (size_emul == 0) turns a normal audio write into a SIGFPE host crash — a guest-triggerable denial of service (CWE-369). Secondary risks (unsigned tail-distance underflow on line 13, and the buf_emul/size_emul consistency the memcpy on line 14 and the clamp on line 9 depend on) all reduce to the same invariant that the mmap region must be a live, correctly-sized allocation. The bounds clamp on line 9 is the real fix and looks correct for buffer overflow, but it does not close the divide-by-zero.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Return-by-value size_t drain count; parameters themselves carry no overflow contract beyond len, handled by line 9 clamp.
Standard QEMU first-member downcast; type-safe because HWVoiceOut is embedded at offset 0 of OSSVoiceOut.
Boolean branch selector; its danger is expressed through the size_emul contract it gates, captured in the line-16 finding.
Load-bearing bounds clamp; safety of the memcpy depends on this being consistent with buf_emul geometry.
Unsigned size_t subtraction that underflows if pos_emul > size_emul; masked by MIN(len,...) but part of the size_emul invariant.
Destination write bounded only by the buf_emul/size_emul/pos_emul consistency contract.
Modulo by size_emul with no non-zero guard — division-by-zero (CWE-369), the primary defect.
POSIX write of a bounded-and-offset region; pos <= total len so advance stays in-bounds; short/error returns handled.
Correct error and transient-busy handling; returns bytes drained so far.
Signed/unsigned comparison is benign because bytes_written is guaranteed >= 0 by the prior check; noted for hygiene only.

findings.title: Division-by-zero on hw->size_emul in the mmap ring wrap
findings.bug_class: Integer division-by-zero (modulo by zero) — SIGFPE / DoS
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0305_cwe369.c (and the QEMU audio/ossaudio.c implementation it models)
findings.invariant: The mmap branch (oss->mmapped) must only be entered when hw->buf_emul is a live allocation and hw->size_emul > 0; equivalently the loop must guard against size_emul == 0 before the modulo (and before the line-13 subtraction).
findings.failure_mode: When hw->size_emul == 0 — mmap setup failed, the emulation buffer was released, or a degenerate zero-sized geometry was negotiated — the `% hw->size_emul` performs an unsigned division by zero on the first loop iteration, raising SIGFPE and terminating the QEMU process. Because the loop is only entered when the line-9 clamp leaves len != 0, a non-zero oss_get_available_bytes with size_emul == 0 is sufficient to trigger it, giving the guest a host-side denial of service.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm every code path that sets oss->mmapped = true also establishes hw->size_emul > 0 and buf_emul non-NULL, and that teardown clears mmapped atomically with size_emul; otherwise add an explicit `if (!hw->size_emul) return 0;` (or return len) before the loop.

findings.title: Unsigned underflow of the ring tail distance size_emul - pos_emul
findings.bug_class: Unsigned integer underflow feeding a copy length
findings.invariant: 0 <= hw->pos_emul < hw->size_emul must hold on entry to every iteration so the subtraction yields a valid in-range tail length.
findings.failure_mode: If pos_emul ever exceeds size_emul (stale cursor after a geometry shrink, or size_emul == 0), the size_t subtraction wraps to a value near SIZE_MAX. The MIN(len, ...) normally caps to_copy back down to the remaining len, so this is not itself an overflow primitive as written — but that mitigation is only as good as the line-9 clamp bounding len to the true free space. The degenerate case size_emul == pos_emul == 0 yields to_copy = 0 and, with len still non-zero, a non-progressing loop, though the line-16 divide-by-zero faults first.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify pos_emul is always reduced modulo size_emul (it is, on line 16, when size_emul > 0) and is reset whenever size_emul changes, so pos_emul <= size_emul is a maintained invariant rather than an assumption.

findings.title: Emulation-buffer memcpy bounded only by buf_emul/size_emul consistency
findings.invariant: hw->buf_emul must point at a live allocation of exactly hw->size_emul bytes, and pos_emul + to_copy <= size_emul must hold, so the copy stays inside the mapped region; buf_emul must be non-NULL whenever the loop runs.
findings.failure_mode: Given the correct triple (buf_emul, size_emul, pos_emul), to_copy is bounded by the line-13 tail distance and this copy is safe. The residual risk is a mismatch: if size_emul overstates the true allocation length of buf_emul, or buf_emul is NULL/stale while size_emul is non-zero, the memcpy writes past the end of (or into a freed/NULL) buffer — a host memory corruption reachable from guest audio traffic. This finding is latent: the arithmetic here is correct, the danger is the setup contract in the code not shown.
findings.what_to_confirm: Trace where buf_emul and size_emul are assigned (the mmap/allocation path) and confirm they are always updated together and torn down together, so size_emul never outlives or exceeds the real buf_emul mapping.

findings.title: Correctness of the available-bytes clamp gating the ring copy
findings.invariant: oss_get_available_bytes(oss) must never report more bytes than can be safely written into buf_emul given its size and current pos_emul; the returned bound must be consistent with the buf_emul ring, not merely with the kernel DMA pointer.
findings.failure_mode: This MIN() is the fix that stops the write from overrunning the mmap region, so its accuracy is security-critical. If oss_get_available_bytes over-reports free space (e.g. it reflects the OSS device's GETOSPACE/play_ptr rather than the buf_emul geometry), `len` stays larger than the ring can hold and the loop can write more total bytes than the buffer across wraps before the cursor re-syncs, producing an out-of-bounds write. As shown, the clamp looks correct; the risk lives in the helper's implementation.
findings.what_to_confirm: Inspect oss_get_available_bytes to confirm it computes free space from the same buf_emul/size_emul/pos_emul state this loop advances, and cannot return a value exceeding size_emul.

audit_checklist: Guarantee hw->size_emul > 0 (and buf_emul non-NULL) on every path that sets oss->mmapped, or add an explicit size_emul==0 guard before the loop, to eliminate the line-16 divide-by-zero.
Confirm pos_emul is maintained strictly less than size_emul and reset whenever the geometry changes, so the line-13 subtraction never underflows.
Trace buf_emul and size_emul allocation/teardown to prove size_emul always equals the real buf_emul mapping length and both change atomically.
Audit oss_get_available_bytes to ensure it cannot over-report free space relative to the buf_emul ring, keeping the line-9 clamp sound.
Verify the `if (bytes_written < 0)` guard remains ahead of the signed/unsigned comparison on line 38 so short-write handling stays correct.
Confirm advance(buf, pos) never produces a pointer past buf + original_len in the non-mmap path.
